verdict_reasoning: HSPA1A is canonically a cytosolic molecular chaperone, but undergoes documented stress-induced translocation to the plasma membrane of stressed and tumor cells, where it embeds directly into the lipid bilayer via selective binding to phosphatidylserine (PS) lipids. Cell surface biotinylation and confocal microscopy confirm outer-leaflet localization on the extracellular face under heat-shock and in tumor cells. The protein has its own anchoring mechanism (direct PS lipid binding) at the PM during the surface state, and reaches the outer face via a well-characterized cellular stress response (heat shock recovery, tumor transformation). Multiple cell-directed therapeutic programs (monoclonal antibodies cmHsp70.1, tumor-penetrating peptides, etc.) target membrane-bound HSPA1A, confirming surface accessibility. Baseline localization is intracellular, but the documented stress-induced PM pool with direct lipid-mediated anchoring qualifies as contextual surface accessibility.
